content: The range of the function $$f(x) = {\log _e}\sqrt {4 - {x^2}} $$ is given by
explanation:
Let $$y = f(x) = {\log _e}\sqrt {4 - {x^2}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \sqrt {4 - {x^2}} = {e^y} \Rightarrow 4 - {x^2} = {e^{2y}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} = 4 - {e^{2y}}$$

$$\because$$ $${x^2} \ge 0 \Rightarrow 4 - {e^{2y}} \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{2y}} \le 4 \Rightarrow 2y \le {\log _e}4$$

$$ \Rightarrow y \le {\log _e}2 \Rightarrow - \infty < y < {\log _e}2$$

range of the function $$ = ( - \infty ,{\log _e}2]$$.

content: The function $$f(x) = \log \left( {{{1 + x} \over {1 - x}}} \right)$$ satisfies the equation
explanation:
$$f(x) = \log \left( {{{1 + x} \over {1 - x}}} \right)$$ (given)(

$$f(y) = \log \left( {{{1 + y} \over {1 - y}}} \right)$$ (by changing x to y)

Taking $$f(x) + f(y)$$

$$ = \log \left( {{{1 + x} \over {1 - x}}} \right) + \log \left( {{{1 + y} \over {1 - y}}} \right) = \log \left( {\left( {{{1 + x} \over {1 - x}}} \right).\left( {{{1 + y} \over {1 - y}}} \right)} \right)$$

$$ = \log \left( {{{1 + xy + x + y} \over {1 - y - x + xy}}} \right) = \log \left( {{{(1 + xy) + (x + y)} \over {(1 + xy) - (x + y)}}} \right)$$

$$ = \log \left( {{{1 + {{x + y} \over {1 + xy}}} \over {1 - {{x + y} \over {1 + xy}}}}} \right) = f\left( {{{x + y} \over {1 + xy}}} \right)$$.

content: The equation ex + x $$-$$ 1 = 0 has, apart from x = 0
explanation:
ex + x $$-$$ 1 = 0

$$\Rightarrow$$ ex = 1 $$-$$ x

Let y = ex, y = 1 $$-$$ x

graph of y = ex and y = 1 $$-$$ x intersect only at point A(0, 1), so they have no other real root except x = 0.

content:
A mapping from IN to IN is defined as follows:

$$f:IN \to IN$$

$$f(n) = {(n + 5)^2},\,n \in IN$$

(IN is the set of natural numbers). Then

options.content: f is both one-to-one and onto
f is one-to-one but not onto

explanation:
$$f(n) = {(n + 5)^2}$$

Let n = n1, n2 $$\in$$ N such that n1 $$\ne$$ n2

$$ \Rightarrow {n_1} + 5 \ne {n_2} + 5 \Rightarrow {({n_1} + 5)^2} \ne {({n_2} + 5)^2} \Rightarrow f({n_1}) \ne f({n_2})$$

$$\therefore$$ f is one-one

$$\because$$ n $$\in$$ N i.e., n = 1, 2, 3, .....

$$\therefore$$ f(1) = 36, f(2) = 49, .....

$$\therefore$$ Range = {f(1), f(2), f(3), .....}

= {36, 49, .....} $$\ne$$ N (codomain)

$$\therefore$$ f is one to one but not onto.

content: The domain of definition of the function $$f(x) = \sqrt {1 + {{\log }_e}(1 - x)} $$ is
explanation:
$$f(x) = \sqrt {1 + {{\log }_e}(1 - x)} $$

Value of f(x) is real when

$$1 + {\log _e}(1 - x) \ge 0$$ and $$1 - x > 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\log _e}(1 - x) \ge - 1$$ and $$x < 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\log _e}(1 - x) \ge {\log _e}{e^{ - 1}}$$ and $$x < 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - x \ge {1 \over e}$$ and $$x < 1 \Rightarrow x \le {{e - 1} \over e}$$ and $$x < 1$$

or, $$x \le {{e - 1} \over e}$$

content: The domain of the function $$f(x) = \sqrt {{{\cos }^{ - 1}}\left( {{{1 - |x|} \over 2}} \right)} $$ is
explanation:
For f(x) to be defined,

$$ - 1 \le {{1 - |x|} \over 2} \le 1$$ & $${\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{1 - |x|} \over 2}} \right) \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 2 \le 1 - |x| \le 2$$ [$$\because$$ $$0 \le {\cos ^{ - 1}}y \le \pi $$ always]

$$ \Rightarrow - 3 \le - |x| \le 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 1 \le |x| \le 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow |x| \le 3$$ [$$\because$$ $$|x| \ge 0$$]

$$\therefore$$ $$x \in [ - 3,3]$$.

content: For what values of x, the function $$f(x) = {x^4} - 4{x^3} + 4{x^2} + 40$$ is monotone decreasing?
explanation:
$$f(x) = {x^4} - 4{x^3} + 4{x^2} + 40$$

$$f'(x) = 4{x^3} - 12{x^2} + 8x = 4x({x^2} - 3x + 2)$$

$$ = 4x(x - 1)(x - 2)$$

From sign scheme and given options, $$f'(x) < 0$$ (for monotonic dec.) is possible if $$1 < x < 2$$.

content: The minimum value of $$f(x) = {e^{({x^4} - {x^3} + {x^2})}}$$ is
The period of the function f(x) = cos 4x + tan 3x is
explanation:
$$\because$$ Period of $$\cos 4x = {{2\pi } \over 4} = {\pi \over 2}$$ & Period of $$\tan 3x = {\pi \over 3}$$

$$\therefore$$ Period of f(x) = L.C.M. of $${\pi \over 2},{\pi \over 3} = \pi $$.

content: The even function of the following is
If f(x + 2y, x $$-$$ 2y) = xy, then f(x, y) is equal to
explanation:
f(x + 2y, x $$-$$ 2y) = xy ....... (i)

Let x + 2y = p, x $$-$$ 2y = q

$$\therefore$$ $$2x = p + q \Rightarrow x = {{p + q} \over 2}$$

Also, $$y = {{p - q} \over 4}$$.

$$\therefore$$ (i) gives $$f(p,q) = {{{p^2} - {q^2}} \over 8}$$ i.e., $$f(x,y) = {{{x^2} - {y^2}} \over 8}$$

content: If $g(f(x))=|\sin x|$ and $f(g(x))=(\sin \sqrt{x})^2$, then
explanation:
Given, $g(f(x))=|\sin x|$

and  $$ f(g(x))=(\sin \sqrt{x})^2 $$

Option (a), if $f(x)=\sin ^2 x$ and $g(x)=\sqrt{x}$,

then  $$ g(f(x))=\sqrt{\sin ^2 x}=|\sin x| $$

and  $$ f(g(x))=(\sin \sqrt{x})^2 $$

Both conditions are satisfied.

content: If $f(x)=\frac{3 x-4}{2 x-3}$, then $f(f(f(x)))$ will be
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Given } f(x)=\frac{3 x-4}{2 x-3} \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} f(f(x)) & =f \frac{(3 x-4)}{(2 x-3)} \\ & =\frac{3\left(\frac{3 x-4}{2 x-3}\right)-4}{2\left(\frac{3 x-4}{2 x-3}\right)-3} \\ & =\frac{3(3 x-4)-4(2 x-3)}{2(3 x-4)-3(2 x-3)} \\ & =\frac{9 x-12-8 x+12}{6 x-8-6 x+9}=\frac{x}{1}=x \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

So, $\quad f(f(x))=x$

Now, $f(f(f(x)))=f(f(f(x)))=f(x)$

Therefore, $f(f(f(x)))=\frac{3 x-4}{2 x-3}$

content: Let $u+v+w=3, u, v, w \in \mathbb{R}$ and $f(x)=u x^2+v x+w$ be such that $f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)+x y$, $\forall x, y \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $f(1)$ is equal to
explanation:
Given $f(x)=u x^2+v x+w, u+v+w=3$ and

$$ f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)=x y . $$

From $f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)+x y$, substitute $x=0$, $y=0$,

$$ f(0)=f(0)+0 \Rightarrow f(0)=0 $$

From $f(x)=u x^2+v x+w, f(0)=w$.

Therefore, $\boldsymbol{w}=\mathbf{0}$.

Substitute $f(x)=u x^2+v x$ into

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)+x y \\ & \Rightarrow u(x+y)^2+v(x+y)=\left(u x^2+v x\right)+\left(u y^2+v y\right)+x y \\ & \Rightarrow u\left(x^2+2 x y+y^2\right)+v x+v y= u x^2+u y^2+v x+v y+xy \\ & \Rightarrow v x^2+2 u x y+u y^2+v x+v y= u x^2 +u y^2+v x+v y+x y \end{aligned} $$

Comparing the coefficient of $x y$ on both sides

$$ 2 u=1 \Rightarrow u=\frac{1}{2} $$

Use the given condition $u+v+w=3$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2}+v+0=3 \\ & v=3-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{2} \end{aligned} $$

The function is $f(x)=\frac{1}{2} x^2+\frac{5}{2} x$.

Calculate $f(1)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} f(l) & =\frac{1}{2}(l)^2+\frac{5}{2}(l) \\ & =\frac{1}{2}+\frac{5}{2}=\frac{6}{2}=3 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ are two polynomials such that $\phi(x)=f\left(x^3\right)+x g\left(x^3\right)$ is divisible by $x^2+x+1$, then

options.content: none of $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ is divisible by $(x-1)$
$g(x)$ is divisible by $(x-1)$ but $f(x)$ is not divisible by $(x-1)$
$f(x)$ io divisible hv $(x-1)$ but $g(x)$ is not divisible by $(x-1)$

explanation:
Given that

$\phi(x)=f\left(x^3\right)+x g\left(x^3\right)$ is divisible by $x^2+x+1$.

The roots of $x^2+x+1$ are $w$ and $w^2$ where $w$ is a complex cube root of unit $\left(w^3=1\right)$

$$ 1+w+w^2=0 $$

Since, $\phi(x)$ is divisible by $x^2+x+1$, we have

$$ \begin{aligned} \phi(w) & =f\left(w^3\right)+w g\left(w^3\right)=f(1)+w g(1)=0 \\ \phi\left(w^2\right) & =f\left(\left(w^2\right)^3\right)+w^3 g\left(\left(w^2\right)^3\right) \\ & =f(1)+w^2 g(1)=0 \end{aligned} $$

Substracting the two equations $g(1)\left(w-w^2\right)=0$.

Since $w \neq w^2$, it implies $g(\mathrm{l})=0$

Substituting $g(1)=0$ into $f(1)+w g(1)=0$, we get $f(\mathrm{l})=0$

Since, $f(1)=0, f(x)$ is divisible by $(x-1)$.

Since $g(1)=0, g(x)$ is divisible by $(x-1)$. because both $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ are divisible by $(x-1)$, it follows that $\phi(1)=f\left(1^3\right)+g\left(1^3\right)$.

$$ \phi(1)=f(1)+g(1)=0+0=0 $$

Therefore, $\phi(x)$ is also divisible by $(x-1)$.

Comparing this with the given options, option (a) is correct.

content: Let $$\mathrm{f}: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$ be a function defined by $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{|x|}-\mathrm{e}^{-x}}{\mathrm{e}^x+\mathrm{e}^{-x}}$$, then

For every real number $$x \neq-1$$, let $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x}{x+1}$$. Write $$\mathrm{f}_1(x)=\mathrm{f}(x)$$ & for $$\mathrm{n} \geq 2, \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{n}}(x)=\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{n}-1}(x)\right)$$. Then $$\mathrm{f}_1(-2) \cdot \mathrm{f}_2(-2) \ldots . . \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{n}}(-2)$$ must be
The equation $$2^x+5^x=3^x+4^x$$ has
In the interval $$( - 2\pi ,0)$$, the function $$f(x) = \sin \left( {{1 \over {{x^3}}}} \right)$$.

options.content: changes sign more than once but finitely many times.

explanation:
Given, $$f(x)=\sin \left(\frac{1}{x^3}\right)$$

and $$-2 \pi< x< 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad-8 \pi^3< x^3< 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad-\frac{1}{8 \pi^3}> \frac{1}{x^3}>-\infty$$

Clearly, $$f(x)$$ will takes values between $$-1$$ and 1 so changes sign infinitely many times.

content: Domain of $$y = \sqrt {{{\log }_{10}}{{3x - {x^2}} \over 2}} $$ is
explanation:
$${\log _{10}}\left( {{{3x - {x^2}} \over 2}} \right) \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{3x - {x^2}} \over 2} \ge {10^0}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{3x - {x^2}} \over 2} \ge 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3x - {x^2} \ge 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 3x + 2 \le 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - 2)(x - 1) \le 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x \in [1,2]$$ ...... (1)

Also, $${{3x - {x^2}} \over 2} > 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 3x < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x(x - 3) < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x \in (0,3)$$ ...... (2)

$$\therefore$$ Intersection of (1) and (2) is $$x \in [1,2]$$

options.content: f does not have a critical point at x = 2
f has a minimum at x = 2
f has neither a maximum nor a minimum at x = 2
f has a minimum at x = 2

explanation:
$$f(x) = {(x - 2)^{17}}{(x + 5)^{24}}$$

$$f'(x) = 17{(x - 2)^{16}}{(x + 5)^{24}} + {(x - 2)^{17}}(24){(x + 5)^{23}}$$

$$ = {(x - 2)^{16}}{(x + 5)^{23}}\{ 17x + 85 + 24x - 48\} $$

$=(x-2)^{16}(x+5)^{23}(41 x+37)=0$

$$\therefore$$ f(x) has neither a maximum nor a minimum at x = 2.

options.content: f(n) and g(n) are not comparable.
$$f(n) > g(n)$$ if n be even and $$f(n) < g(n)$$ if n be odd.

content: The maximum value of $$f(x) = {e^{\sin x}} + {e^{\cos x}};x \in R$$ is
explanation:
We have $$f(x) = {e^{\sin x}} + {e^{\cos x}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{{e^{\sin x}} + {e^{\cos x}}} \over 2} \ge {e^{\sqrt {\sin x + \cos x} }}$$

$${{{e^{\sin x}} + {e^{\cos x}}} \over 2} \ge {e^{ - {{\sqrt 2 } \over 2}}}$$

$${e^{\sin x}} + {e^{\cos x}} \ge 2{e^{ - {{\sqrt 2 } \over 2}}} \ge 2{e^{ - {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}}}$$

Thus, maximum value of $$f(x)$$ is $$2{e^{1/\sqrt 2 }}$$.

content: $$f:X \to R,X = \{ x|0 < x < 1\} $$ is defined as $$f(x) = {{2x - 1} \over {1 - |2x - 1|}}$$. Then

options.content: f is neither injective nor surjective

explanation:
Given, $$f(x) = {{2x - 1} \over {1 - |2x - 1|}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$f(x) = \left\{ {\matrix{ {{{2x - 1} \over {1 + 2x - 1}},} & {0 < x < {1 \over 2}} \cr {{{2x} \over {1 - 2x + 1}},} & {{1 \over 2} \le x < 1} \cr } } \right.$$

$$ = \left\{ {\matrix{ {{{2x - 1} \over {2x}},} & {0 < x < {1 \over 2}} \cr {{{2x - 1} \over {2 - 2x}},} & {{1 \over 2} \le x < 1} \cr } } \right.$$

Now, let us draw the graph of above function.

From the above graph it is clear that f(x) is one-one and since the range of f(x) is real number, which implies that codomain is equal to range.

$$\therefore$$ f(x) is one-one and onto both and hence f(x) is bijective.

content: Let f : R $$\to$$ R be given by f(x) = | x<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ 1 |, x$$\in$$R. Then,

options.content: f has a local minimum at x = $$\pm$$ 1 but no local maximum.
f has a local minimum at x = 0 but no local minimum.
f has a local minima at x = $$\pm$$ 1 and a local maxima at x = 0.
f has neither a local maxima nor a local minima at any point.

explanation:
Given, f(x) = | x2 $$-$$ 1 |, x$$\in$$R. The graph of f(x) is clearly, from graph.


f(x) has a local minima at x = $$\pm$$ 1 and has local maxima at x = 0.

content: f(x) is real valued function such that 2f(x) + 3f($$-$$x) = 15 $$-$$ 4x for all x$$\in$$R. Then f(2) =
explanation:
Given, 2f(x) + 3f($$-$$x) = 15 $$-$$ 4x ..... (i)

On replacing x by $$-$$x in Eq. (i), we get

2f($$-$$x) + 3f(x) = 15 + 4x ..... (ii)

On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

f(x) = 4x + 3

$$\therefore$$ f(2) = 4 $$\times$$ 2 + 3 = 11

content: Consider the functions f<sub>1</sub>(x) = x, f<sub>2</sub>(x) = 2 + log<sub>e</sub> x, x > 0. The graphs of the functions intersect

options.content: once in (0, 1) and once in (e, e<sup>2</sup>)

explanation:
Given, f1(x) = x, f2(x) = 2 + loge x

Now, let h(x) = f2(x) $$-$$ f1(x)

= (2 + loge x) $$-$$ x

= 2 + loge x $$-$$ x

Here, h(0+) < 0, h(1) > 0, h(e) > 0 and h(e2) < 0 and value of h(x) for all x $$\ge$$ e2 is negative.

$$\therefore$$ h(x) = 0 has two roots is (0, 1) and (e, e2)

content: Given that f : S $$\to$$ R is said to have a fixed point at c of S if f(c) = c. Let f : [1, $$\infty$$) $$\to$$ R be defined by f(x) = 1  + $$\sqrt x $$. Then

options.content: f has no fixed point in [1, $$\infty$$)
f has unique fixed point in [1, $$\infty$$)
f has to fixed points in [1, $$\infty$$)
f has infinitely many fixed points in [1, $$\infty$$)

explanation:
We have,

$$f(x) = 1 + \sqrt x $$

$$f(c) = 1 + \sqrt c $$

$$ \Rightarrow c = 1 + \sqrt c $$ [$$\because$$ $$f(c) = c$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {(c - 1)^2} = {(\sqrt c )^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {c^2} - 2c + 1 = c$$

$$ \Rightarrow {c^2} - 3c + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow c = {{3 \pm \sqrt 5 } \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ f has unique fixed point in [1, $$\infty$$]

content: Let $$f(x) = 1 - \sqrt {({x^2})} $$, where the square root is to be taken positive, then

options.content: f has no extrema at x = 0

content: The domain of $$f(x) = \sqrt {\left( {{1 \over {\sqrt x }} - \sqrt {x + 1} } \right)} $$ is
explanation:
Given, $$f(x) = \sqrt {\left( {{1 \over {\sqrt x }} - \sqrt {x + 1} } \right)} $$

f(x) is defined $${1 \over {\sqrt x }} - \sqrt {x + 1} \ge 0$$ and x > 0

$${x^2} + x - 1 \le 0$$, x > 0

$$x = {{ - 1 \pm \sqrt 5 } \over 2}$$, x > 0

$$ \therefore $$ Domain of f(x) is $$\left( {0,{{\sqrt 5 - 1} \over 2}} \right]$$.

content: Let $$A = \{ x \in R: - 1 \le x \le 1\} $$ and $$f:A \to A$$ be a mapping defined by $$f(x) = x\left| x \right|$$. Then f is
Let $$f(x) = \sqrt {{x^2} - 3x + 2} $$ and $$g(x) = \sqrt x $$ be two given functions. If S be the domain of fog and T be the domain of gof, then
explanation:
We have,

$$f(x) = \sqrt {{x^2} - 3x + 2} $$

$$g(x) = \sqrt x $$

$$fog = f(g(x)) = \sqrt {x - 3\sqrt x + 2} $$

fog is defined $$x - 3\sqrt x + 2 \ge 0$$ and $$x \ge 0$$

$$(\sqrt x - 1)(\sqrt x - 2) \ge 0$$

$$x \in ( - \infty ,1] \cup [4,\infty )$$

$$gof = g(f(x)) = \sqrt {\sqrt {{x^2} - 3x + 2} } $$

gof is defined $${x^2} - 3x + 2 \ge 0$$

$$(x - 1)(x - 2) \ge 0$$

$$x \in ( - \infty ,1] \cup [2,\infty )$$

$$ \therefore $$ Domain of fog is $$( - \infty ,1] \cup [4,\infty )$$

Domain of gof is $$( - \infty ,1] \cup [2,\infty )$$

$$ \therefore $$ S $$ \cap $$ T is an interval.

content: Let $$f:R \to R$$ be defined by $$f(x) = {x^2} - {{{x^2}} \over {1 + {x^2}}}$$ for all $$x \in R$$. Then,

options.content: f is one-one but not onto mapping
f is onto but not one-one mapping
f is both one-one and onto
f is neither one-one nor onto

explanation:
We have,

$$f(x) = {x^2} - {{{x^2}} \over {1 + {x^2}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$f( - x) = {( - x)^2} - {{{{( - x)}^2}} \over {1 + {{( - x)}^2}}}$$

$$ = {x^2} - {{{x^2}} \over {1 + {x^2}}} = f(x)$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$f( - x) = f(x)$$

So, f(x) is many one

Again, $$f(x) = {x^2} - {{{x^2}} \over {1 + {x^2}}}$$

$$ = {{{x^2} + {x^4} - {x^2}} \over {1 + {x^2}}} = {{{x^4}} \over {1 + {x^2}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Range of f(x) will be [0, $$\infty $$)

But, co-domain of f(x) = R

$$ \therefore $$ Range $$ \ne $$ co-domain. So, f(x) is onto.

content: Consider the function f(x) = cos x<sup>2</sup>. Then,
explanation:
We have,

f(x) = cos x2

Let T be the period of f(x). Then

f(x + T) = f(x)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ cos(x + T)2 = cos x2

But there is no value of T for which

cos(x + T)2 = cos x2

$$ \therefore $$ f(x) is not periodic.

content: Let a > b > 0 and I(n) = a<sup>1/n</sup> $$-$$ b<sup>1/n</sup>, J(n) = (a $$-$$ b)<sup>1/n</sup> for all n $$ \ge $$ 2, then
explanation:
Let a = 4, b = 1 and n = 2

$$ \therefore $$ $$I(n) = {a^{1/n}} - {b^{1/n}}$$

$$ = {4^{1/2}} - {1^{1/2}} = 2 - 1 = 1$$

and $$J(n) = {(a - b)^{1/n}}$$

$$ = {(4 - 1)^{1/2}} = \sqrt 3 $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$J(n) > I(n)$$

Similarly, we can prove for other values of a, b, n.

content: The domain of definition of $$f(x) = \sqrt {{{1 - |x|} \over {2 - |x|}}} $$ is

options.content:
$$[ - 1,1] \cup (2,\infty )$$

Here (a, b) $$ \equiv $$ {x : a < x < b} and [a, b] $$ \equiv $$ {x : a $$ \le $$ x $$ \le $$ b}

explanation:
We have, $$f(x) = \sqrt {{{1 - |x|} \over {2 - |x|}}} $$

f(x) is defined for all x satisfying

$${{1 - |x|} \over {2 - |x|}}\, \ge \,0 \Rightarrow {{|x| - 1} \over {|x| - 2}}\, \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \,|x|\, \le 1$$ or $$|x|\, > 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow x \in [ - 1,1]$$ or $$x \in ( - \infty , - 2) \cup (2,\infty )$$

$$ \Rightarrow x \in ( - \infty , - 2) \cup [ - 1,1] \cup (2,\infty )$$

content: If f : R $$ \to $$ R be defined by f (x) = e<sup>x</sup> and g : R $$ \to $$ R be defined by g(x) = x<sup>2</sup>. The mapping gof : R $$ \to $$ R be defined by (gof) (x) = g[f(x)] $$\forall $$x$$ \in $$R. Then,

options.content: gof is bijective but f is not injective.
gof is injective but g is injective
gof is injective but g is not bijective
gof is surjective and g is surjective

explanation:
We have f : R $$ \to $$ R, defined by f(x) = ex and g : R $$ \to $$ R defined by g(x) = x2

Now we have,

(g) (x) = g(f(x))

= g(ex)

=(ex)2

= e2x, $$\forall $$x$$ \in $$ R

$$ \Rightarrow $$ gof is injective and g is neither injective nor surjective.

$$ \Rightarrow $$ gof is injective but g(x) is not bijective.

content: For 0 $$ \le $$ p $$ \le $$ 1 and for any positive a, b; let I(p) = (a + b)<sup>p</sup>, J(p) = a<sup>p</sup> + b<sup>p</sup>, then

options.content: I(p) < J(p) in $$\left[ {0,{p \over 2}} \right]$$ and I(p) > J(p) in $$\left[ {{p \over 2},\infty } \right]$$
I(p) < J(p) in $$\left[ {{p \over 2},\infty } \right]$$ and I(p) > J(p) in $$\left[ {0,{p \over 2}} \right]$$

explanation:
Here, let $$p = {1 \over m}$$

then $${({a^p} + {b^p})^{1/p}} = {({a^{1/m}} + {b^{1/m}})^m}$$

$$ = a + b + k,k \ge 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${a^p} + {b^p} \ge {(a + b)^p} \ge (a + b)$$

$$ \Rightarrow J(p) \ge I(p)$$

content: Let $$f:R \to R$$ be such that f is injective and $$f(x)f(y) = f(x + y)$$ for $$\forall x,y \in R$$. If f(x), f(y), f(z) are in G.P., then x, y, z are in

options.content: AP depending on the value of x, y, z
GP depending on the value of x, y, z

explanation:
Since, $$f:R \to R$$ is injective and $$f(x)f(y) = f(x + y)$$, $$\forall x,y \in R$$.

$$ \therefore $$$$f(x) = {a^x}$$

Again, f(x), f(y), f(z) are in GP.

$$ \Rightarrow {(f(y))^2} = f(x)\,.\,f(y)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^{2y}} = {a^x}\,.\,{a^z}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^{2y}} = {a^{x + z}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2y = x + z$$

$$ \therefore $$ x, y, z are in AP.

options.content: only one positive and only two negative real roots
not more than one real root
has two positive nd one negative real rot

explanation:
We have,

$$f(x) = {x^{13}} + {x^{11}} + {x^9} + {x^7} + {x^5} + {x^3} + x + 19$$

$$ \Rightarrow f'(x) = 13{x^{12}} + 11{x^{10}} + 9{x^8} + 7{x^6} + 5{x^4} + 3{x^2} + 1$$

$$ \therefore $$ f'(x) has no real root.

$$ \therefore $$ f(x) = 0 has not more than one real root.

content: Show that sin x is a monotonic increasing function of x in 0 < x < $$\pi$$/2.
explanation:
Let x1, x2 be two values of x in the given interval of 0 < x < $$\pi$$/2, where x1 < x2

$$\therefore$$ $${{{x_2} - {x_1}} \over 2}$$ and $${{{x_2} + {x_1}} \over 2}$$ both will lie between 0 and $$\pi$$/2

$$\therefore$$ $$f({x_2}) - f({x_1}) = \sin {x_2} - \sin {x_1}$$

$$ = 2\cos {{{x_1} + {x_2}} \over 2}.\sin {{{x_2} - {x_1}} \over 2}$$

Now, $${{{x_1} + {x_2}} \over 2}$$ and $${{{x_2} - {x_1}} \over 2}$$ both being acute angles, both sine and cosine of them are +ve.

$$\therefore$$ $$f({x_2}) - f({x_1}) > 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$f({x_2}) > f({x_1})$$, where x2 > x1.

$$\therefore$$ sin x is a monotonic increasing function in 0 to $$\pi$$/2.

content: The function $$\mathrm{f}: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$ defined by $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{e}^x+\mathrm{e}^{-x}$$ is :
explanation:
Let's analyze the function $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{e}^x+\mathrm{e}^{-x}$$ where $$\mathrm{f}: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$.

First, we check if the function is one-one (injective).

An injective function means that if $$\mathrm{f}(a) = \mathrm{f}(b)$$, then $$a = b$$. Let's assume $$\mathrm{f}(a) = \mathrm{f}(b)$$.

So, we have:

$$ \mathrm{e}^a + \mathrm{e}^{-a} = \mathrm{e}^b + \mathrm{e}^{-b} $$

Let's rewrite the equation as:

$$ \mathrm{e}^a + \frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^a} = \mathrm{e}^b + \frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^b} $$

Given that the exponential function $$\mathrm{e}^x$$ is strictly increasing, and $$\mathrm{e}^{-x}$$ is strictly decreasing, the sum is minimized when $$x = 0$$. This involves checking various values, but combining these two observations, we can see that the function is symmetric and not strictly monotonic. Therefore, it is not one-one.

Next, we check if the function is onto (surjective).

A surjective function means that for every $$y \in \mathbb{R}$$, there exists an $$x \in \mathbb{R}$$ such that $$\mathrm{f}(x) = y$$. Here, considering $$ \mathrm{f}(x) = \mathrm{e}^x + \mathrm{e}^{-x} $$, let's find the range.

Define a new function $$g(x) = \mathrm{e}^x + \mathrm{e}^{-x}$$. We use the fact that:

$$ g(0) = 2 $$

When $$x \rightarrow \infty$$, then $$ \mathrm{e}^x \rightarrow \infty $$ and $$ \mathrm{e}^{-x} \rightarrow 0 $$, so $$ g(x) \rightarrow \infty $$.

When $$x \rightarrow -\infty$$, then $$ \mathrm{e}^x \rightarrow 0 $$ and $$ \mathrm{e}^{-x} \rightarrow \infty $$, so $$ g(x) \rightarrow \infty $$.

But at no point does the function $$ \mathrm{e}^x + \mathrm{e}^{-x} $$ produce negative values. Therefore, the range of $$ \mathrm{f}(x) \geq 2$$. Hence, the function is not surjective.

Given that the function is neither injective nor surjective, it cannot be bijective.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option D: not bijective

content: Choose the correct statement :

options.content: $$x+\sin 2 x$$ is not a periodic function

explanation:
To determine whether the functions are periodic, we need to see if there exists a positive constant $ T $ such that $ f(x + T) = f(x) $ for all $ x $ in the domain of the function. Let's analyze each option one by one.

Option A: $ x + \sin 2x $ is a periodic function

The function $ x + \sin 2x $ consists of a linear term $ x $ and a periodic term $ \sin 2x $. The linear term $ x $ is not periodic because it continuously increases or decreases without repeating any values. Therefore, the sum $ x + \sin 2x $ cannot be periodic because the non-periodic linear term dominates the behavior of the function as $ x $ increases.

Option B: $ x + \sin 2x $ is not a periodic function

As explained above, the function $ x + \sin 2x $ is not periodic due to the presence of the linear term $ x $. Thus, this statement is correct.

Option C: $ \cos (\sqrt{x} + 1) $ is a periodic function

To determine the periodicity of $ \cos (\sqrt{x} + 1) $, we need to check if there exists a positive constant $ T $ such that $ \cos (\sqrt{x + T} + 1) = \cos (\sqrt{x} + 1) $ for all $ x $. However, the argument of the cosine function, $ \sqrt{x} + 1 $, is not periodically repeating as $ x $ increases. The function $ \sqrt{x} $ increases without bound, thus the argument of the cosine function does not repeat its values periodically.

Option D: $ \cos (\sqrt{x} + 1) $ is not a periodic function

As explained above, the argument $ \sqrt{x} + 1 $ does not repeat periodically, so $ \cos (\sqrt{x} + 1) $ is not a periodic function. Thus, this statement is correct.

Therefore, the correct statements are:

Option B: $ x + \sin 2x $ is not a periodic function

Option D: $ \cos (\sqrt{x} + 1) $ is not a periodic function

content: Let $$f(x) = {x^2} + x\sin x - \cos x$$. Then

options.content: f(x) = 0 has at least one real root
f(x) = 0 has at least one positive root
f(x) = 0 has at least one negative root

explanation:
Given $$f(x) = {x^2} + x\sin x - \cos x$$

Now, $$f'(x) = {d \over {dx}}({x^2}) + {d \over {dx}}(x\sin x) - {d \over {dx}}(\cos x)$$

$$f'(x) = 2x + x\cos x + \sin x + \sin x$$

$$ \Rightarrow f'(x) = 2x + x\cos x + 2\sin x$$

$$ \Rightarrow f(x)$$ is an increasing function.

Now, $$f(0) = - 1$$

$$f(\infty ) = + \,\infty $$

$$f( - \infty ) = \, + \,\infty $$

$$\because$$ $$f( - \infty ) = + \mathrm{ve}$$ and $$f(0) = - \mathrm{ve}$$

$$\therefore$$ Therefore must be at least one negative root in $$( - \infty ,0)$$.

$$\because$$ $$f(0) = - \mathrm{ve}$$ and $$f(\infty ) = + \mathrm{ve}$$

$$\therefore$$ Then, must be at least one p[ositive root in (0, $$\infty$$).

content: Let p(x) be a polynomial with real co-efficient, p(0) = 1 and p'(x) > 0 for all x $$\in$$ R. Then

options.content: p(x) has at least two real roots
p(x) has only one positive real root
p(x) may have negative real root
p(x) has infinitely many real roots

explanation:
$$P(0) = 1$$

$$P'(x) > 0;\forall x \in R$$

Let $$P(x) = {x^3} + 1$$

$$P'(x) = 3{x^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ P(x) may have always be increasing as shown in above.

P(x) may have negative real root.

explanation: We have, f and g be periodic function with periods T<sub>1</sub> and T<sub>2</sub> respectively f + g is periodic if T<sub>1</sub> = T<sub>2</sub>

content: Consider the function $$y = {\log _a}(x + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} ),a > 0,a \ne 1$$. The inverse of the function
explanation:
Given, $$y = {\log _a}(x + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} ),a > 0,a \ne 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^y} = (x + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} )$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^{ - y}} = {1 \over {x + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}$$

$$ = \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^y} - {a^{ - y}} = 2x \Rightarrow x = {{{a^y} - {a^{ - y}}} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {f^{ - 1}}(y) = {{{e^{y\log a}} - {e^{ - y\log a}}} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {f^{ - 1}}(y) = \sinh (y\log a)$$ $$ \because $$ $$\left( {{{{e^x} - {e^x}} \over 2} = \sinh (x)} \right)$$

content: Let f : X $$ \to $$ X be such that f [f(x)] = x, for all x$$\in$$X and X$$ \subseteq $$R, then

f is onto but not one-to-one

Now, f$$-$$1(x) = f(x)

i.e. f(x) is bijective.
